passages.title: Voyage of Going: Beyond the Blue Line
passages.source_html:
Voyage of Going: Beyond the Blue Line 


A


One feels a certain sympathy for Captain James Cook on the day in 1778 that he ‘discovered’ Hawaii. Then on his third expedition to the Pacific, the British navigator had explored scores of islands across the breadth of the sea, from lush New Zealand to the lonely wastes of Easter Island. This latest voyage had taken him thousands of miles north from the Society Islands to an archipelago so remote that even the old Polynesians back on Tahiti knew nothing about it. Imagine Cook’s surprise, then, when the natives of Hawaii came paddling out in their canoes and greeted him in a familiar tongue, one he had heard on virtually every mote of inhabited land he had visited. Marveling at the ubiquity of this Pacific language and culture, he later wondered in his journal: ‘How shall we account for this Nation spreading itself so far over this Vast Ocean?’ 


B


Answers have been slow in coming. But now a startling archaeological find on the island of Éfaté, in the Pacific nation of Vanuatu, has revealed an ancient seafaring people, the distant ancestors of today’s Polynesians, taking their first steps into the unknown. The discoveries there have also opened a window into the shadowy world of those early voyagers. At the same time, other pieces of this human puzzle are turning up in unlikely places. Climate data gleaned from slow-growing corals around the Pacific and from sediments in alpine lakes in South America may help explain how, more than a thousand years later, a second wave of seafarers beat their way across the entire Pacific. 


C


‘What we have is a first or second-generation site containing the graves of some of the Pacific’s first explorers,’ says Spriggs, professor of archaeology at the Australian National University and co-leader of an international team excavating the site. It came to light only by luck. A backhoe operator, digging up topsoil on the grounds of a derelict coconut plantation, scraped open a grave – the first of dozens in a burial ground some 3,000 years old It is the oldest cemetery ever found in the Pacific islands, and it harbors the bones of an ancient people archaeologists call the Lapita, a label that derives from a beach in New Caledonia where a landmark cache of their pottery was found in the 1950s. They were daring blue-water adventurers who roved the sea not just as explorers but also as pioneers, bringing along everything they would need to build new lives – their families and livestock, taro seedlings and stone tools. 


D


Within the span of a few centuries the Lapita stretched the boundaries of their world from the jungle-clad volcanoes of Papua New Guinea to the loneliest coral outliers of Tonga, at least 2,000 miles eastward in the Pacific. Along the way they explored millions of square miles of unknown sea, discovering and colonizing scores of tropical islands never before seen by human eyes: Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Fiji, Samoa. 


E


What little is known or surmised about them has been pieced together from fragments of pottery, animal bones, obsidian flakes, and such oblique sources as comparative linguistics and geochemistry. Although their voyages can be traced back to the northern islands of Papua New Guinea, their language – variants of which are still spoken across the Pacific – came from Taiwan. And their peculiar style of pottery decoration, created by pressing a carved stamp into the cay, probably had its roots in the northern Philippines. With the discovery of the Lapita cemetery on Éfaté, the volume of data available to researchers has expanded dramatically. The bones of at least 62 individuals have been uncovered so far – including old men, young women, even babies – and more skeletons are known to be in the ground Archaeologists were also thrilled to discover six complete Lapita pots. ‘It’s an important find,’ Spriggs says, for it conclusively identifies the remains as Lapita, ‘It would be hard for anyone to argue that these aren’t Lapita when you have human bones enshrined inside what is unmistakably a Lapita urn.’ 


F


Several lines of evidence also undergird Spriggs’s conclusion that this was a community of pioneers making their first voyages into the remote reaches of Oceania. For one thing, the radiocarbon dating of bones and charcoal places them early in the Lapita expansion. For another, the chemical makeup of the obsidian flakes littering the site indicates that the rock wasn’t local; instead it was imported from a large island in Papua New Guinea’s Bismarck Archipelago, the springboard for the Lapita’s thrust into the Pacific. A particularly intriguing clue comes from chemical tests on the teeth of several skeletons. DNA teased from these ancient bones may also help answer one of the most puzzling questions in Pacific anthropology: Did all Pacific islanders spring from one source or many? Was there only one outward migration from a single point in Asia, or several from different points? ‘This represents the best opportunity we’ve had yet,’ says Spriggs, ‘to find out who the Lapita actually were, where they came from, and who their closest descendants are today.’ 


G


There is one stubborn question for which archaeology has yet to provide any answers: How did the Lapita accomplish the ancient equivalent of a moon landing, many times over? No one has found one of their canoes or any rigging, which could reveal how the canoes were sailed Nor do the oral histories and traditions of later Polynesians offer any insights, for they segue into myth long before they reach as far back in time as the Lapita. ‘All we can say for certain is that the Lapita had canoes that were capable of ocean voyages, and they had the ability to sail them,’ says Geoff Irwin, a professor of archaeology at the University of Auckland and an avid yachtsman. Those sailing skills, he says, were developed and passed down over thousands of years by earlier mariners who worked their way through the archipelagoes of the western Pacific making short crossings to islands within sight of each other. Reaching Fiji, as they did a century or so later, meant crossing more than 500 miles of ocean, pressing on day after day into the great blue void of the Pacific. What gave them the courage to launch out on such a risky voyage? 


H


The Lapita’s thrust into the Pacific was eastward, against the prevailing trade winds, Irwin notes. Those nagging headwinds, he argues, may have been the key to their success. ‘They could sail out for days into the unknown and reconnoiter, secure in the knowledge that if they didn’t find anything, they could turn about and catch a swift ride home on the trade winds. It’s what made the whole thing work.’ Once out there, skilled seafarers would detect abundant leads to follow to land: seabirds and turtles, coconuts and twigs carried out to sea by the tides, and the afternoon pileup of clouds on the horizon that often betokens an island in the distance. Some islands may have broadcast their presence with far less subtlety than a cloud bank. Some of the most violent eruptions anywhere on the planet during the past 10,000 years occurred in Melanesia, which sits nervously in one of the most explosive volcanic regions on Earth. Even less spectacular eruptions would have sent plumes of smoke billowing into the stratosphere and rained ash for hundreds of miles. It’s possible that the Lapita saw these signs of distant islands and later sailed off in their direction, knowing they would find land for returning explorers, successful or not, the geography of their own archipelagoes provided a safety net to keep them from overshooting their home ports and sailing off into eternity. 


I


However they did it, the Lapita spread themselves a third of the way across the Pacific, then called it quits for reasons known only to them. Ahead lay the vast emptiness of the central Pacific, and perhaps they were too thinly stretched to venture farther. They probably never numbered more than a few thousand in total, and in their rapid migration eastward they encountered hundreds of islands – more than 300 in Fiji alone. Still, more than a millennium would pass before the Lapita’s descendants, a people we now call the Polynesians, struck out in search of new territory.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1-7 


Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1? In boxes 1-7 on your answer sheet, write YES if the statement is true NO if the statement is false NOT GIVEN if the information is not givern in the passage


1

Captain cook once expected the Hawaii might speak another language of people from other pacific islands.

正确答案： A. YES

原文答案句：Imagine Cook’s surprise, then, when the natives of Hawaii came paddling out in their canoes and greeted him in a familiar tongue, one he had heard on virtually every mote of inhabited land he had visited.

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是“詹姆斯·库克船长曾期望夏威夷人可能会说来自其他太平洋岛屿的另一种语言。”

定位关键信息：通过题目中的“Captain Cook”、“Hawaii”和“another language”可以定位到文章A段中描述库克船长发现夏威夷时，夏威夷人用一种熟悉的语言向他打招呼的句子。

解题思路：原文中提到库克船长对夏威夷人使用一种他熟悉的语言感到惊讶，这表明他之前预期夏威夷人可能会说一种不同的语言。因此，题目中的陈述与文章信息一致，答案为YES。


2

Captain cook depicted number of cultural aspects of Polynesians in his journal.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：he later wondered in his journal: ‘How shall we account for this Nation spreading itself so far over this Vast Ocean?’

对应段落：A段

审题：题目问的是“詹姆斯·库克在他的日记中描绘了波利尼西亚人的许多文化方面。”

定位关键信息：利用题目中的关键词"Captain Cook", "journal", "cultural aspects of Polynesians"定位到A段中关于库克日记的描述。

解题思路：原文中库克在日记中只提到了对波利尼西亚人分布范围的疑问（'How shall we account for this Nation spreading itself so far over this Vast Ocean?'），并未提及任何关于波利尼西亚人文化方面的描述。因此，题目中的说法与原文信息不符，答案为NO。


3

Professor Spriggs and his research team went to the Efate to try to find the site of ancient cemetery.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：It came to light only by luck. A backhoe operator, digging up topsoil on the grounds of a derelict coconut plantation, scraped open a grave – the first of dozens in a burial ground some 3,000 years old.  

对应段落：C段  

审题：题目问Spriggs教授和他的研究团队是否去了Efate试图寻找古代墓地。  

定位关键信息：利用题目中的"Professor Spriggs"、"Efate"和"ancient cemetery"定位到C段，该段提到墓地遗址的发现过程。  

解题思路：原文明确指出墓地遗址是偶然发现的（"It came to light only by luck"），由挖掘机操作员偶然挖出，而非Spriggs团队主动寻找的结果。题目声称Spriggs团队是主动去寻找墓地，与原文事实相反，因此答案为NO。


4

The Lapita completed a journey of around 2,000 miles in a period less than a centenary.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：Within the span of a few centuries the Lapita stretched the boundaries of their world from the jungle-clad volcanoes of Papua New Guinea to the loneliest coral outliers of Tonga, at least 2,000 miles eastward in the Pacific.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是拉皮塔人是否在不到一个世纪的时间里完成了大约2000英里的旅程。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"2,000 miles"和"time period"可以定位到D段中描述拉皮塔人扩张的句子。

解题思路：原文提到拉皮塔人在"几个世纪"内从巴布亚新几内亚扩张到汤加，距离至少2000英里。题目中的"less than a centenary"（不到一个世纪）与原文的"few centuries"（几个世纪）不符，因为几个世纪远不止一个世纪。因此，时间信息不符，应该选NO


5

The Lapita were the first inhabitants in many pacific islands.

正确答案： A. YES

原文答案句：Along the way they explored millions of square miles of unknown sea, discovering and colonizing scores of tropical islands never before seen by human eyes: Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Fiji, Samoa.

对应段落：D段

审题：题目问的是拉皮塔人是否是许多太平洋岛屿的第一批居民。

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"the first inhabitants"和"pacific islands"可以定位到D段中提到拉皮塔人探索并殖民了数以百计的热带岛屿，这些岛屿以前从未被人类所见。

解题思路：根据原文中的句子"discovering and colonizing scores of tropical islands never before seen by human eyes"可以确定拉皮塔人是这些岛屿的第一批居民，因此题目的陈述与文章信息一致，答案为YES。


6

The unknown pots discovered in Efate had once been used for cooking.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

原文答案句： With the discovery of the Lapita cemetery on Éfaté, the volume of data available to researchers has expanded dramatically. The bones of at least 62 individuals have been uncovered so far – including old men, young women, even babies – and more skeletons are known to be in the ground Archaeologists were also thrilled to discover six complete Lapita pots.

对应段落：E段

审题：题目问的是“在Efate发现的未知陶器曾被用于烹饪”这一说法是否与文章信息一致。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的“unknown pots discovered in Efate”来定位答案。在E段中找到关于Efate发现的陶器的描述。

解题思路：原文只是提到发现了的6个陶罐，并没有提到这些陶器曾被用于烹饪。因此，题目中的说法在文章中没有提及，答案为NOT GIVEN。


7

The urn buried in Éfaté site was plain as it was without any decoration.

正确答案： B. NO

原文答案句：It would be hard for anyone to argue that these aren’t Lapita when you have human bones enshrined inside what is unmistakably a Lapita urn.  

对应段落：E段  

审题：题目问“在Éfaté遗址中埋葬的瓮是朴素的，因为它没有任何装饰”这一说法是否正确。  

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词“Éfaté site”和“urn”定位到D段，找到关于瓮的描述。  

解题思路：原文强调这是一个“ unmistakably a Lapita urn”（确凿无疑的拉皮塔陶瓮）。而拉皮塔陶器的定义性特征就是其独特的装饰风格（peculiar style of pottery decoration）。因此，这个瓮不可能是“plain”（朴素无装饰）的，答案为NO。


Questions 8-10 


Complete the summary below. Choose NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS from the passage for each answer. Write your answers in boxes 8-10 on your answer sheet.


8

Scientific evident found in Efate site Tests show the human remains and the charcoal found in the buried um are from the start of the Lapita period. Yet The 8

正确答案： 8 rock
Question 8

原文答案句：The chemical makeup of the obsidian flakes littering the site indicates that the rock wasn’t local; instead it was imported from a large island in Papua New Guinea’s Bismarck Archipelago, the springboard for the Lapita’s thrust into the Pacific.  

对应段落：F段  

审题：题目问的是在Efate遗址中发现的科学证据，其中提到了人类遗骸和木炭，以及一个空格需要填写的内容。  

定位关键信息：通过题目中的关键词"human remains"和"charcoal"可以定位到F段前半部分，然后继续阅读发现后半部分提到了"rock"的相关信息。  

解题思路：原文中明确提到"the chemical makeup of the obsidian flakes"（黑曜石碎片的化学成分），这对应了题目中的"Tests show the human remains and the charcoal found in the buried um are from the start of the Lapita period."（测试显示在埋葬的坛子里发现的人类遗骸和木炭来自拉皮塔时期的开始）。接着原文提到"the rock wasn’t local"（岩石不是当地的），这对应了题目中的空格部分。因此，空格处应填写"rock"。


9

covering many of the Efate site did not come from that area. Then examinations carried out on the 9

正确答案： 9 teeth
Question 9

原文答案句：A particularly intriguing clue comes from chemical tests on the teeth of several skeletons.  

对应段落：G段  

审题：题目要求根据给出的摘要内容，从文章中找出Efate遗址中的许多物品并非来自当地，然后通过检查某种身体部位来获取信息。  

定位关键信息：利用题目中的"Efate site"和"did not come from that area"定位到G段，该段提到Efate遗址中发现的物品（如黑曜石碎片）并非当地产物，而是从别处进口的。继续阅读该段，找到与"examinations"和身体部位相关的句子。  

解题思路：根据原文G段的内容，前文提到遗址中的黑曜石碎片并非当地产物，是从别处进口的。接着提到"chemical tests on the teeth of several skeletons"（对几具骨骼牙齿的化学测试）是另一个重要的线索。题目中的"Then examinations carried out on"对应原文中的"chemical tests on"，而"teeth"是测试的对象，因此答案为"teeth"。


10

discovered at Efate site reveal that not everyone buried there was a native living in the area. In fact, DNA could identify the Lapita’s nearest 10 present-days.

正确答案： 10 descendants
Question 10

原文答案句：‘This represents the best opportunity we’ve had yet,’ says Spriggs, ‘to find out who the Lapita actually were, where they came from, and who their closest descendants are today.’

对应段落：E段

审题：题目要求根据摘要填空，找出Efate遗址的发现揭示了Lapita的最近现代后代是谁。

定位关键信息：利用题目中的Efate site和descendants这两个关键词，在文章中定位到E段最后一句，该句明确提到“who their closest descendants are today”。

解题思路：原文E段最后一句直接回答了题目要求的信息，即通过DNA测试可以确定Lapita的最近现代后代是谁，原文中的“descendants”与题目空格前的“nearest”相呼应，因此答案为descendants。


Questions 11-13 


Answer the questions below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER from the passage for each answer.


11

11 What did the Lapita travel in when they crossed the oceans?

正确答案： 11 canoes
Question 11

原文答案句：No one has found one of their canoes or any rigging, which could reveal how the canoes were sailed.

对应段落：G段

审题：题目问Lapita在横渡海洋时乘坐的是什么。

定位关键信息：根据题目中的关键词"Lapita"和"crossed the oceans"定位到文章中描述Lapita航海的段落。在G段中找到明确提到"Lapita"和"canoes"的句子。

解题思路：原文G段提到"no one has found one of their canoes or any rigging, which could reveal how the canoes were sailed"，说明Lapita使用的是"canoes"（独木舟）进行航海。虽然原文提到"no one has found"，但结合上下文可以确定他们确实乘坐的是独木舟。题目问的是他们乘坐的交通工具，因此答案为"canoes"。


12

12 In Irwins’s view, what would the Latipa have relied on to bring them fast back to the base?

正确答案： 12 trade winds
Question 12

原文答案句：  

‘They could sail out for days into the unknown and reconnoiter, secure in the knowledge that if they didn’t find anything, they could turn about and catch a swift ride home on the trade winds. It’s what made the whole thing work.’  

对应段落：  H段  

审题：  

题目问的是“根据Irwin的观点，拉皮塔人依靠什么快速返回基地？”，需要找出拉皮塔人在航行中用于快速返回的依据。  

定位关键信息：  

通过题目中的“in Irwin’s view”和“bring them fast back to the base”可以定位到D段，其中提到了Irwin对拉皮塔人航行策略的分析。原文中明确提到“they could turn about and catch a swift ride home on the trade winds”，即他们依靠贸易风快速返回。  

解题思路：  

原文中Irwin指出，拉皮塔人向东航行时逆风而行，但依靠贸易风可以快速返回。题目问的是他们依靠什么快速返回基地，对应原文中的“trade winds”，即贸易风。因此，答案为“trade winds”。


13

13 Which sea creatures would have been an indication to the Lapita of where to find land ?

正确答案： 13 seabirds and turtles
Question 13

原文答案句：Once out there, skilled seafarers would detect abundant leads to follow to land: seabirds and turtles, coconuts and twigs carried out to sea by the tides, and the afternoon pileup of clouds on the horizon that often betokens an island in the distance

对应段落：H段  

审题：题目问的是拉皮塔人可以通过哪些海洋生物来判断哪里有陆地。  

定位关键信息：利用题目中的关键词"sea creatures"和"indication"定位到文章中描述拉皮塔人航行技能和如何寻找陆地的内容。  

解题思路：原文I段提到拉皮塔人通过海洋生物来判断陆地位置，原文句子"Once out there, skilled seafarers would detect abundant leads to follow to land: seabirds and turtles, coconuts and twigs carried out to sea by the tides, and the afternoon pileup of clouds on the horizon that often betokens an island in the distance"明确指出拉皮塔人通过"seabirds and turtles"等海洋生物来判断陆地位置。因此答案为seabirds and turtles。
